critic_raw:
ASSESSOR 1:
Verdict: critique_wins

The paper's headline WAPE improvement rests on a compromised comparison. The seasonal heuristic lacks both promotional inputs and current calibration. Particularly damaging: the paper notes the gap is largest during promotions, which is exactly when the information asymmetry is most pronounced. No intermediate model was tested. Recommend rejection of the gap estimate as evidence of gradient-boosting superiority.
defender_raw:
ASSESSOR 2:
Verdict: critique_wins

All three problems (information gap, regime miscalibration, absent intermediate baseline) are clearly present in the study description. The operational justification offered by the authors addresses a different question (does this beat the live system?) than what the accuracy gap claims (is gradient boosting superior?). Both questions matter but require different evaluation designs. The study only partially answers either.

ASSESSOR 3:
Verdict: critique_wins

The scenario description reveals the flaws explicitly — the promotion calendar withholding and the never-re-parameterized expansion note are admissions of methodological limitations. The gap between 11.8% and 18.9% WAPE cannot be attributed cleanly to the modeling approach when the comparators differ in both inputs and calibration quality.
adjudication_raw: All three assessors independently converge on critique_wins. The issues are not empirically uncertain — they are structural design problems explicitly acknowledged in the paper's own methods section. No empirical test is needed to establish these as flaws. The verdict is clear: critique_wins.
